The pattern that separates a 20-minute incident from a 4-hour one appears before the second dashboard loads.

10 min read
Just now

--

Eighty-three production incidents.
I have been keeping count since the first one that genuinely scared me. A payment processing failure at 2am that I was the primary responder for, six weeks into my first on-call rotation, with no senior engineer available and a CEO who had somehow joined the bridge within four minutes of the alert firing.
That incident took three hours and forty minutes to resolve.
The root cause was a single environment variable that had not been set correctly in the production deployment. The fix was one line. The diagnosis was three hours and thirty-eight minutes of searching in the wrong place because I had no framework for finding the right place.
I have thought about that night consistently since. Not because it was the worst incident I’ve been part of. Because it was the one that made the pattern visible.
Every incident since has confirmed the same observation.
The first five minutes determine almost everything that follows.

What the First Five Minutes Actually Are
Most engineers treat the first five minutes of a production incident as orientation time.
Join the bridge. Open the dashboards. Pull up the logs. Get your bearings. Start looking.
This is not wrong. It is not what the first five minutes are for.
The first five minutes are the only window in the entire incident where you can define the search space before the investigation begins. After five minutes, the investigation has momentum. Engineers are following threads. Hypotheses have formed. The group has organized around specific areas. Changing direction becomes socially and cognitively expensive.
The engineers who resolve incidents fastest use the first five minutes to define the search space. The engineers who resolve incidents slowest use the first five minutes to start searching in the full space.
Both groups are working hard. One group is working in a bounded space. The other is working in an unbounded one.
The gap between twenty minutes and four hours is almost entirely the gap between those two approaches.

What the First Five Minutes Reveal About the Incident
Every production incident has three properties that are visible in the first five minutes if you look for them and invisible if you don’t.
The first property is scope. What is affected and what is not? This is not a question about what is wrong. It is a question about the shape of what is wrong.
Is this one service or multiple services? One region or all regions? All users or a specific subset? All endpoints or specific ones? All requests or requests above a certain size or complexity?
Each scope question eliminates categories of failure. A failure affecting multiple services simultaneously cannot be caused by a bug in one service’s code. A failure affecting all regions simultaneously cannot be caused by a region-specific infrastructure change. A failure affecting all users cannot be caused by a bug in a code path that only some users reach.
The eliminations happen before anyone reads a log. The search space shrinks before the investigation begins.
Most engineers skip the scope questions because they feel like overhead. The incident is happening. You should be doing something. Asking whether this is one region or multiple regions feels like slowing down when you should be speeding up.
It is the opposite. The scope questions are the fastest thing you can do in the first five minutes. They take ninety seconds and they eliminate hours of searching in the wrong place.
The second property is timeline. When did this start relative to what changed?
The alert firing time is not the incident start time. Alerts fire at thresholds. The incident started before the threshold was crossed. The incident started when the first anomaly appeared in the metrics, which is almost always before the alert fired.
The timeline question is not when did the alert fire. It is when did the metrics first move in the wrong direction. The answer is often ten to thirty minutes before the alert. The cause started then, not when the phone rang.
What changed in the window immediately before the metrics started moving? Deployment. Configuration change. Infrastructure update. Scheduled job. Traffic pattern shift. Certificate expiration. Credential rotation. Feature flag change.
The timeline does not always identify the cause. It almost always narrows the space to a manageable set of candidates. A failure that correlates with a deployment has a fundamentally different starting hypothesis than a failure that started forty minutes before any change.
The third property is signal character. What kind of failure signal is this?
Connection refused is a different failure character than connection timeout. A climbing error rate is a different character than a stable error rate at a high level. An error that affects every request is a different character than an error that affects one in a thousand requests.
Signal character points at mechanism. Connection refused means something is actively refusing the connection, not failing to respond. The set of things that actively refuse connections is small and specific. Timeout means something is slow or unresponsive. The set of things that cause slowness is large and nonspecific.
Signal character narrows the hypothesis space by pointing at the class of mechanism that can produce the observed signal.
Scope, timeline, signal character. Three properties. Ninety seconds to establish all three. The search space that results is a fraction of the full space.
The investigation that follows is not smarter. It is smaller.

The 83 Incidents, Categorized
Across eighty-three incidents, I have observed that the causes fall into a small number of categories with reliable frequency.
This is not a universal law. It is a pattern strong enough to be useful.
Resource exhaustion accounts for roughly a quarter of the incidents I have been part of. Connection pool exhaustion. Thread pool exhaustion. Memory heap exhaustion. Disk space exhaustion. File descriptor limit exhaustion. The signature is gradual onset, metrics trending wrong before the alert fires, and a resource that is at or near its configured maximum. The question that finds it: when did this metric last look clean, and what is its current value relative to its configured maximum?
Dependency failure accounts for another significant portion. An external service, a database, a message queue, a third-party API, something the application depends on stopped working or started working incorrectly. The signature is sudden onset, errors that appear across multiple services that share the dependency, and error messages that mention connection or authentication to an external system. The question that finds it: what do all the failing requests have in common in terms of what they call downstream?
Configuration change accounts for a category that is systematically underestimated. Not code deployments. Configuration changes. Feature flag flips. Environment variable changes. Infrastructure configuration updates. Certificate rotations. Scheduled configuration refreshes. The signature is sudden onset correlated with a non-code change, affects all users immediately rather than gradually, and produces errors that are specific rather than generic. The question that finds it: what changed in the thirty minutes before the metrics started moving that was not a code deployment?
Cascade amplification is the category most likely to be misdiagnosed because the visible symptoms look like a different problem. One service degrades. Upstream services start retrying. The retries add load to the already degrading service. The service degrades further. More retries. The incident looks like a traffic spike or a DDoS. The cause is your own retry logic. The signature is traffic to the failing service increasing as it degrades rather than decreasing, and multiple upstream services showing retry behavior simultaneously. The question that finds it: is the traffic to the failing component increasing or decreasing as the failure gets worse?
Gradual accumulation is the category that produces the longest investigations because the symptoms are diffuse and the cause started long before the alert. A queue growing slowly over hours. A connection pool leaking slowly. A cache filling and evicting aggressively. The signature is metrics that have been moving in the wrong direction for a long time before the threshold was crossed, and an alert that fired well after the problem started. The question that finds it: how long has this metric been trending in the wrong direction, and what started the trend?
These five categories are not exhaustive. They cover enough ground that establishing which category an incident belongs to in the first five minutes eliminates most hypotheses before anyone investigates anything.

The Bridge Conversations That Determine the Outcome
The first five minutes of a production incident are a social environment as much as a technical one.
Engineers are joining. Someone is establishing who is the incident commander. Someone is pulling the affected service’s dashboard. Someone is looking at the deployment history. Someone is already calling the database team.
In this environment, the engineers who are asking scope, timeline, and signal character questions are swimming against the current of the bridge. The current is toward action. Toward investigation. Toward doing something visible.
The scope questions look passive. The timeline questions look like delay. The signal character question looks like pedantry when the service is clearly down and every second matters.
The fastest incident commanders I have watched have internalized something that takes years to accept intuitively.
The first five minutes of a production incident are not when you solve the problem. They are when you define what solving looks like. Spending those five minutes defining the search space rather than starting the search is the highest-leverage action available on the bridge.
The conversation that produces a good first five minutes sounds like this.
“Before we start pulling logs, let me ask three things. Is this affecting all users or a subset? Is this one region or multiple? And what does the error signature look like specifically — connection refused, timeout, or something else?”
Ninety seconds. Three answers. The bridge now has a constraint map. Every investigation thread that follows operates inside a bounded space rather than an unbounded one.
The conversation that produces a poor first five minutes sounds like this.
Everyone opens their own dashboard. Nobody asks the scope questions because everyone is already looking. The investigation sprawls. Four engineers are following four different threads in four different directions. Two of them are in the wrong place.
Twenty minutes later, one of the threads finds something. The bridge reconverges. The other three threads are abandoned.
That reconvergence is where the time was lost. The twenty minutes of parallel searching in the wrong place. The scope questions would have prevented most of it.

The Moment That Changes Everything
Across eighty-three incidents, there is a moment that appears in almost every one of them.
The moment when someone asks the question that changes the direction of the investigation.
In most incidents, that question comes too late. Twenty minutes into the investigation, someone asks a question that would have been the right first question. The answer to that question changes where everyone looks. The root cause is found shortly after.
The question is almost always a scope, timeline, or signal character question. What do all the failing requests have in common? When did this start relative to the configuration change? Is the error rate climbing or stable?
The pattern across eighty-three inci
